clinical_variables.primary_condition: Colorectal Adenocarcinoma of the Cecum (Stage II)

narrative:
When 70‑year‑old Martin K. first told his daughter that he’d been “feeling a bit off” it sounded like a minor flare of his long‑standing ulcerative colitis. He’s had pancolitis since his early 40s and has worked as a book‑keeper, but for the past six months he had been noticing subtle changes: his stools were darker than usual, sometimes tinged with a faint reddish hue, and he lost about 10 pounds without trying. He also described a gnawing pressure in the lower right abdomen and a fatigue that made his usual brisk walks feel like running uphill. He didn’t view these complaints as anything beyond the “age‑related aches” that come with obesity and hypertension.

The first visit (Day 0) was with his primary care physician (PCP). Routine labs revealed a mild normocytic anemia (Hgb 11.4 g/dL), borderline hyperglycemia (HbA1c 7.2 %), and an elevated fecal calprotectin at 300 µg/g, a known marker of active colonic inflammation. His PCP suspected a UC flare and prescribed a short course of prednisone and a tapering plan for mesalamine. He was also advised to schedule a colonoscopy, citing the new-onset GI bleeding, which had never occurred before.

Colonoscopic evaluation (Day 28) was technically straightforward given his prior endoscopies. The scope revealed a 4 cm polypoid lesion in the medial aspect of the cecum, partially covered in inflammatory pseudopolyps. The surface eroded slightly, and it raised a suspicion of neoplasia. Five biopsies were obtained, and the pathologist later reported a moderate‑grade tubular adenocarcinoma, T2 (invading the muscularis propria) with negative margins in the superficial biopsies. At this point, Martin understood that he was dealing with a colorectal cancer, not just UC flare. The diagnosis spurred a rapid cascade of investigations.

An abdominal computed tomography (CT) scan with contrast (Day 35) confirmed the endoscopic findings: a localized mass in the cecum, with no regional lymphadenopathy and no distant metastatic lesions. The gastroenterologist determined that Martin’s tumor was appropriately a Stage II (pT2N0M0) cancer. He was referred to a surgical oncologist, and a multidisciplinary tumor board reviewed his case. Given his comorbidities—type 2 diabetes with peripheral neuropathy, well‑controlled hypertension (BP 135/80 under lisinopril), obesity (BMI 33.5), and a 30‑pack‑year smoking history—there was concern about operative risk, especially in the setting of uncontrolled inflammation from UC. The board decided on a planned elective right hemicolectomy, but only after a short period of preoperative optimization of his diabetes and a smoking cessation counseling session.

Preoperative optimization (Days 36–46) included adjustment of his metformin dose and initiation of a low‑dose basal insulin. He was prescribed a high‑protein, low‑fat diet to combat his weight loss. His family history—his father succumbed to colorectal cancer at 68—also made the team uneasy; they felt urgent intervention was warranted.

The right hemicolectomy (Day 48) was performed laparoscopically, with the surgical team employing a protective ileostomy due to Martin’s high intraoperative risk profile. Postoperatively, Martin’s recovery was protracted. On Post‑Op day 3, he developed a superficial surgical site infection (SSI) that required percutaneous drainage on day 5 and a brief course of vancomycin. The ileostomy output was large, and his metabolic demands increased, leading to hyperglycemia that peaked at 250 mg/dL on day 7. He also reported a worsening of abdominal pain, localized to the right lower quadrant, which the surgical team thought was typical ileitis; anti‑inflammatory therapy was increased. By day 10, Martin was still febrile (38.4°C) and had a leukocytosis of 12.5 × 10⁹/L. A repeat ultrasound ruled out abscess, and an infectious disease consult determined that the SSI had likely seeded into the peritoneal cavity—though the peritoneal cultures grew no organism. This warranted a longer course of intravenous antibiotics.

His ICU stay lasted 4 days, during which he was weaned off ventilatory support. By postoperative day 14, he was ambulating, but his appetite remained poor. The oncology team reviewed his pathology: 4 mm tumor budding, low differentiation, microsatellite-stable tumor, and a tumor lysis syndrome‑like picture on labs. They concluded that adjuvant chemotherapy was not indicated for Stage II if there were no adverse features. However, because of his high risk factors (age, obesity, UC), the team opted for a 4‑cycle adjuvant capecitabine to be initiated when he could tolerate oral intake.

Martin left the hospital on day 18 with wound care instructions and an appointment in 2 weeks with his surgeon and oncology nurse for follow‑up. The ileostomy was planned to be reversed within 8 weeks post‑surgery, contingent upon wound healing and metabolic stabilization.

The next clinical milestone (Day 35 after discharge, approx. Day 53 from initial symptoms) marked Martin’s first post‑discharge visit. His wound was healing, but his stoma output remained excessive. At the oncology visit, he reported increasing fatigue, new-onset diarrhea, and a sensation of “tightness” in his abdomen. On physical exam, there was a palpable firm, indurated mass in the right lower quadrant that moved with defecation. His labs were now deranged: hemoglobin had dropped to 9.7 g/dL, and his CRP was 18 mg/L. A CT scan showed a recurrence of the cecal lesion that had now invaded the adjacent terminal ileum, with an enlarged, suspicious para‑mesenteric node. There was no distant metastasis at that point.

On an urgent tumor board meeting (Day 64), the consensus was that Martin’s cancer had progressed to Stage III (pT3N1M0). Because of his frailty, comorbidities, and postoperative complications, a full adjuvant chemotherapy regimen (FOLFOX) was deemed intolerable. The surgical team recommended a palliative right hemicolectomy with resection of the involved terminal ileum and an end‑to‑end anastomosis, but this carried a high peritoneal seeding risk. Instead, they suggested a definitive chemoradiation, albeit with the knowledge that the benefit in colorectal cancer is limited without nodal involvement.

Martin and his daughter debated the options. He declined aggressive surgery, citing his recent complications and poor physical resilience. Instead, the oncology team initiated a modest palliative chemotherapy with capecitabine and bevacizumab, combined with supportive care for pain and nutrition. His appetite remained poor, and he experienced intermittent severe crampy abdominal pains. Over the next two months (Days 65–115), his condition steadily deteriorated. His hemoglobin fell to 8.3 g/dL, requiring two packed RBC transfusions. He developed worsening dyspnea and intermittent lower‑extremity edema, attributed to the progression of local disease and his chronic heart disease.

On the 120th day after the initial presentation, Martin’s condition had worsened to a point where the oncologist felt it appropriate to shift the focus from disease‑directed therapy to comfort measures. Hospice care was initiated. He returned to his home, with his daughter by his side, as the disease progressed. He passed away a few weeks later (Day 150 from initial symptoms), unable to cope with the cumulative burden of colorectal recurrence, uncontrolled diabetes, lingering hyperinflammatory UC flare, and the lingering scarring of his previous surgical sites.

Throughout this trajectory, Martin’s family history, lifestyle factors, and the dual pressures of chronic inflammatory bowel disease and metabolic derangements contributed to a course of colorectal cancer that went from a local Stage II tumor to an aggressive recurrence that overwhelmed his ability to tolerate aggressive therapy. The narrative underscores how a seemingly “curable” early colorectal cancer can still follow a grim arc when comorbidities, advanced age, and a pro‑inflammatory backdrop coexist.

timeline.reason_for_visit: Feeling off, lower abdominal pressure, stool changes
timeline.note:
Chief Complaint: 70‑year‑old male presents with vague fatigue, lower right abdominal pressure, and darker stools over the past six months, accompanied by a 10‑lb unintentional weight loss. 
History of Present Illness: Mr. Martin reports feeling generally fatigued and experiencing a pressure sensation in the right lower quadrant. He notes darker stools with occasional faint red tint but denies frank rectal bleeding. He has a 12‑year history of type 2 diabetes, well‑controlled on metformin, hypertension on lisinopril, and obesity (BMI 33.5). He has a 30‑pack‑year smoking history and a family history of colorectal cancer (father, age 68). He has ulcerative colitis (pancolitis) diagnosed at age 36 and denies recent medication adherence. 
Past Medical History: Ulcerative colitis, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obesity. 
Medications: Metformin 500 mg BID, lisinopril 10 mg daily. 
Allergies: None. 
Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue, weight loss, and altered stool color. No abdominal pain beyond mild pressure, no nausea/vomiting, no fever, or rectal bleeding. 
Physical Examination: Vitals: BP 138/82, HR 88, Temp 98.4 °F, RR 16, SpO₂ 98% on room air. General: warm, alert, no acute distress. Abdomen: mild tenderness in the right lower quadrant; no rebound or guarding, no masses. No stigmata of malnutrition. 
Labs: CBC shows mild normocytic anemia (Hb 11.4 g/dL, Hct 34%); CMP is within normal limits. HbA1c 7.2% indicates borderline hyperglycemia. Fecal calprotectin is elevated at 300 µg/g, supporting active colonic inflammation. 
Assessment: 1) Ulcerative colitis flare – clinical presentation and fecal calprotectin suggest active pancolitis. 2) Possible occult GI bleeding or colonic lesion given dark stools and family history of colorectal cancer; colonoscopy is indicated to rule out malignancy or severe mucosal disease. 3) Mild anemia likely anemia of chronic disease or chronic GI blood loss. 4) Moderately controlled diabetes – HbA1c 7.2% needs monitoring. 
Plan: 
- Initiate prednisone 40 mg PO daily, taper over 2 weeks to minimize steroid exposure. 
- Start mesalamine 4 g PO BID to control colonic inflammation. 
- Counsel patient on adherence, steroid side‑effect monitoring, and importance of early reporting of worsening abdominal pain, bleeding, or fever. 
- Schedule colonoscopy within 4–6 weeks to evaluate mucosal inflammation, rule out neoplasia, and assess bleeding source. 
- Repeat CBC and CMP in 2 weeks to monitor anemia and renal function, and repeat fecal calprotectin in 6 weeks to gauge response. 
- Follow‑up clinic visit in 2 weeks to assess symptom improvement and medication tolerance. 
- Discuss lifestyle modifications; advise smoking cessation support, dietician referral for weight management, and diabetes education. 
- Educate about signs of complications: severe abdominal pain, persistent bleeding, high fever, or signs of steroid toxicity. 
Disposition: Discharged home with prescriptions, follow‑up appointments, and instructions for urgent care if symptoms worsen.

timeline.reason_for_visit: Colonoscopy for evaluation of dark stools and abdominal pain
timeline.note:
Chief Complaint: Right lower quadrant pressure, dark stools, 10‑lb weight loss, fatigue. 
Reason for visit: follow‑up after colonoscopy for active ulcerative colitis.

History of Present Illness:
70‑year‑old male with known pancolitis on mesalamine 4 g BID and prednisone 40 mg daily (taper underway). He reports persistent right lower quadrant pressure, intermittent dark tarry stools, 10‑lb weight loss over the last 6 months, and persistent fatigue. The colonoscopy performed on 2025‑01‑25 revealed diffuse erythema, erosions, and a 4 cm polypoid lesion in the cecum partially covered by inflammatory pseudopolyps. Five biopsy specimens were taken. Patient is anxious awaiting pathology.

Past Medical History: Ulcerative colitis (pan‑colitis), Type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obesity.

Medications: Metformin 1000 mg BID, Lisinopril 20 mg daily, Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, Prednisone 40 mg daily (taper underway), Mesalamine 4 g BID.

Allergies: NKDA.

Review of Systems:
Positive for abdominal pain (right lower quadrant pressure), anemia (black stools), weight loss, fatigue. Negative for fever, chills, or rectal bleeding other than melena. No recent medication changes.

Physical Examination:
Vitals stable (BP 136/80 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 98.5 °F, RR 16 /min, SpO2 98 %). Abdominal exam: soft, non‑tender, no distension. No new abdominal tenderness since colonoscopy. Bowel sounds normal. No external signs of infection.

Assessment:
1) Active ulcerative colitis flare.
2) Cecal polypoid lesion – likely inflammatory pseudopolyp with areas of mucosal erosion; differential includes low‑grade dysplasia or adenoma.
Awaiting histology.

Plan:
1) Continue mesalamine 4 g BID.
2) Continue prednisone taper as per schedule; goal to complete taper by 2025‑02‑28.
3) Re‑evaluate in 2 weeks after pathology results.
4) Monitor CBC, CMP, and liver function for steroid and mesalamine effects at each visit.
5) Educate patient on increased bleeding risk with oral steroids; advise to report any sudden increase in melena or hematochezia.
6) If pathology reveals dysplasia or adenoma, discuss endoscopic polypectomy or surgical consultation.
7) Encourage diet rich in fiber and adequate fluid intake.
8) Schedule routine follow‑up in 3 months for UC activity if asymptomatic.

Disposition:
Patient will return to the GI clinic once pathology results are available.

timeline.note:
Chief Complaint: Post‑colonoscopy staging and oncology referral.

History of Present Illness: 70‑year‑old male presents for discussion of colonoscopy biopsy results and staging CT. He reports persistent right lower quadrant (RLQ) pain, dark stools, 10‑lb weight loss, and fatigue over the past 6 months. He has been on prednisone 40 mg daily (taper ongoing) and mesalamine 4 g BID for active ulcerative colitis. He is concerned about the 4‑cm mass identified on imaging.

Past Medical History: Ulcerative colitis (pancolitis), Type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obesity, former smoker.

Medications: Metformin 1000 mg BID, Lisinopril 20 mg daily, Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, Prednisone 40 mg daily (taper ongoing), Mesalamine 4 g BID.

Allergies: None.

Physical Examination: Vitals stable – BP 134/78, HR 82, T 98.6 °F, RR 17, SpO₂ 97%. Abdomen soft, non‑distended, RLQ tenderness 3/10, no rebound or guarding. No palpable masses.

Imaging: Contrast CT abdomen/pelvis – 4 cm localized mass in right colon, no regional lymphadenopathy or distant metastasis.

Assessment:
1. Possible adenocarcinoma of the cecum/ascending colon. 2. Active ulcerative colitis with possible inflammatory mass.
3. No evidence of regional lymph node disease or metastatic spread.

Plan:
- Elective laparoscopic right hemicolectomy after pre‑operative optimization.
- Continue current ulcerative colitis regimen; plan for prednisone taper and mesalamine maintenance.
- Initiate pre‑operative diabetes optimization: discuss insulin initiation or adjustment of metformin to avoid peri‑operative hyper/hypoglycemia.
- Schedule pre‑operative surgical oncology consult for 2025-02-12.
- Order baseline labs (CBC, CMP, LFTs, HbA1c) before surgery.
- Educate patient on signs of infection, GI bleeding, and when to seek urgent care.
- Continue antihyperlipidemic and antihypertensive therapy unchanged.
- Arrange follow‑up in clinic 2 weeks prior to surgery to review labs and optimize nutrition.

The patient was informed of the findings, risks, and benefits of surgery. He understood the urgency, expressed willingness to proceed, and agreed to the optimization plan.

timeline.visit_scenario: The pathologist had reported a moderate‑grade tubular adenocarcinoma, T2, with negative margins in superficial biopsies. The gastroenterologist confirmed a Stage II (pT2N0M0) colorectal adenocarcinoma localized to the cecum. A contrast‑enhanced CT abdomen was ordered to assess for regional lymphadenopathy and distant metastasis. The CT performed on day 35 (2025-02-12) confirmed a localized 4‑cm cecal mass, no regional nodes, and no distant metastases. The oncologic multidisciplinary team reviewed the case: given Martin’s age, type 2 diabetes with neuropathy, ongoing UC inflammation, hypertension, obesity, and smoking history, the team expressed concern about operative risks. Nevertheless, consensus was to proceed with elective laparoscopic right hemicolectomy after a short period of pre‑operative diabetes optimization and smoking cessation counseling.

timeline.note:
---
**Chief Complaint:** Pre‑operative diabetes optimization

**History of Present Illness:**
Mr. Martin, 70‑year‑old male with type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obesity, and pancolitis, presents for pre‑operative endocrine optimization prior to scheduled right hemicolectomy on 2025‑02‑18. He reports ongoing fatigue, increased thirst, and polyuria, but denies new gastrointestinal complaints. He has been on insulin glargine 20 units daily plus metformin 1000 mg BID. Recent labs reveal HbA1c 7.2% and fasting glucose 148 mg/dL.

**Review of Systems:**
- Constitutional: fatigue
- Genitourinary: polyuria, increased thirst
- GI: no new symptoms
- Cardiovascular: no chest pain, stable BP
- Respiratory: no SOB

**Past Medical History:**
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus
- Hypertension
- Obesity (BMI 33.5)
- Ulcerative colitis, pancolitis, on prednisone taper (40 mg daily, taper pending)
- Active colitis flare; recent colonoscopy with 4‑cm right colon mass

**Past Surgical History:**
- None prior to planned hemicolectomy

**Medications:**
- Metformin 1000 mg BID (prior), now 500 mg BID
- Insulin glargine 20 units daily (prior), now 10 units nightly
- Lisinopril 20 mg daily
- Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly
- Mesalamine 4 g BID
- Prednisone 40 mg daily (taper pending)
- Cefazolin 1 g IV (pre‑op prophylaxis)
- Nicotine patch 1 patch/day (prescribed for smoking cessation)

**Allergies:** None known.

**Social History:**
- Former smoker, 40 pack‑year history; currently using nicotine patch.
- No alcohol or illicit drug use.

**Vitals:**
- BP: 132/74 mmHg
- HR: 80 bpm
- Temp: 98.6 °F
- RR: 15/min
- SpO₂: 98% on room air
- BMI: 33.5

**Physical Exam:**
- General: alert, in no acute distress
- HEENT: normal
- Cardiovascular: regular rate/rhythm, no murmurs
- Respiratory: clear to auscultation
- Abdominal: soft, nondistended, no tenderness
- Extremities: no pedal edema, peripheral pulses palpable
- Neurologic: grossly intact

**Assessment:**
1. Type 2 diabetes mellitus – currently suboptimally controlled (HbA1c 7.2%, fasting glucose 148 mg/dL). Peri‑operative hyperglycemia risk is elevated due to ongoing prednisone therapy and surgical stress.
2. Hypertension – well controlled on lisinopril.
3. Obesity – BMI 33.5 contributes to insulin resistance.
4. Ulcerative colitis – active flare on prednisone taper; risk of infection with peri‑operative antibiotics.
5. Smoking – high peri‑operative risk; nicotine patch prescribed.

**Plan:**
- **Diabetes management:**
  - Reduce metformin to 500 mg BID (to minimize GI side effects and renal safety pre‑op). 
  - Initiate basal insulin glargine 10 units nightly (titrated from 20 units daily) to improve insulin sensitivity and achieve peri‑operative glucose targets of 80–180 mg/dL.
  - Provide patient education on carbohydrate counting, insulin timing, and hypoglycemia awareness.
  - Arrange dietitian consult to increase protein intake and reduce simple sugars.
- **Blood pressure:** Continue lisinopril 20 mg; monitor BP during pre‑op period.
- **Prednisone taper:** Continue 40 mg daily until 3 days pre‑op, then taper to 20 mg for 2 days with goal of <20 mg by surgery day.
- **Smoking cessation:** Prescribe nicotine patch; reinforce counseling.
- **Pre‑operative labs:** Repeat fasting glucose and HbA1c one week pre‑op; obtain CMP to confirm normal renal function.
- **Surgical coordination:** Confirm timing of right hemicolectomy (2025‑02‑18) and ensure antibiotic prophylaxis (cefazolin 1 g IV) per surgical protocol.
- **Education:** Discuss expected peri‑operative glucose fluctuations, insulin adjustments, and monitoring.

**Follow‑up recommendations:**
- Return for diabetes follow‑up 1 week prior to surgery (2025‑02‑11) to re‑measure fasting glucose and adjust insulin dose as needed.
- Continue dietary modifications and nicotine patch until at least surgery day.
- Post‑operative endocrine follow‑up within 48 hrs after surgery to adjust insulin and review glucose logs.
- Coordinate with surgical oncology for operative day medication schedule.
- If HbA1c or fasting glucose are markedly elevated (>200 mg/dL), delay surgery and repeat labs.

---

timeline.visit_scenario: During the pre‑operative assessment, the endocrinologist reviewed Martin’s glycemic control: HbA1c 7.2%, fasting glucose ~155 mg/dL. To improve insulin sensitivity and reduce perioperative risk, the clinician reduced metformin to 500 mg BID and added basal insulin glargine 10 units nightly. The patient was instructed on carbohydrate counting and medication timing, and a dietary consult was arranged to increase protein intake and reduce simple sugars. Blood pressure was stable on lisinopril. Smoking cessation counseling was reiterated, and a nicotine patch was prescribed. The plan was to re‑assess glycemic control 1 week pre‑op.
timeline.patient_response: He felt relieved to have a concrete plan and committed to dietary changes. Concerns about insulin side effects were discussed and addressed.
timeline.disease_progression_notes: Prepared for surgery by optimizing glycemic control: metformin dose reduced, basal insulin added. No change in tumor status.

timeline.reason_for_visit: Elective laparoscopic right hemicolectomy with protective ileostomy
timeline.note:
**Chief Complaint:** Elective laparoscopic right hemicolectomy with protective loop ileostomy.

**History of Present Illness:**
A 70‑year‑old male with a long history of pancolitis, T2DM, HTN, obesity, and well‑controlled medications underwent elective laparoscopic right hemicolectomy with creation of a protective loop ileostomy on 2025‑02‑18. Resection margins were clear. Post‑operative course was complicated by a superficial surgical site infection (SSI) noted on POD 3 with erythema and drainage. Percutaneous drainage of the incision was performed on POD 5 and empiric vancomycin 1 g IV BID was started for 5 days. Ileostomy output remained high, contributing to hyperglycemia with a peak glucose of 250 mg/dL on POD 7. Insulin and metformin doses were adjusted accordingly. The patient developed a flare of ulcerative colitis (low‑grade fever 38.4 °C, leukocytosis 12.5×10⁹/L) on POD 10; mesalamine was increased and a small‑dose prednisone taper was initiated. A repeat abdominal ultrasound on POD 10 excluded an abscess. Infectious disease (ID) consulted and recommended a longer course of IV antibiotics due to concern for seeding of the peritoneal cavity. The patient spent 4 days in ICU and was transferred to the surgical ward once stabilized. By POD 14 the patient ambulated with support and was discharged home with wound care and nutrition instructions.

**Past Medical History:** Ulcerative colitis (pancolitis), Type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obesity.

**Medications (pre‑discharge):**
- Metformin 500 mg BID
- Insulin glargine 10 units nightly
- Lisinopril 20 mg daily
- Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly
- Prednisone 40 mg daily (taper ongoing: 40 mg day 1‑3, 30 mg day 4‑7, 20 mg day 8‑10, 10 mg day 11‑14)
- Mesalamine 4 g BID (increased to 4 g TID for flare)
- Cefazolin 1 g IV pre‑op
- Vancomycin 1 g IV BID (currently 5 days)
- Nicotine patch

**Allergies:** NKDA

**Vitals (Day 14):** BP 140/80, HR 96, T 37.8 °C, RR 18, SpO₂ 97 %

**Physical Examination:**
- Incision: tibial, erythematous, drainage present.
- Ileostomy: high output, no abdominal guarding or peritonitis.
- Right lower quadrant: mild tenderness without rebound.

**Labs and Tests:**
- CBC: WBC 12.5×10⁹/L
- BMP: within normal limits
- CRP: 20 mg/L
- Blood cultures: negative
- Peritoneal fluid culture: negative
- Ultrasound: no abscess

**Assessment and Plan:**
1. **Post‑operative superficial SSI with potential peritoneal seeding.**
   - Continue IV antibiotic coverage: Vancomycin 1 g IV BID (extend to 14 days total) plus Ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily for broad coverage.
   - Re‑evaluate wound drainage daily; repeat ultrasound if clinical deterioration.
2. **Diabetes mellitus exacerbated by ileostomy output.**
   - Increase insulin glargine to 15 units nightly; add NPH 10 units BID if glucose >200 mg/dL.
   - Counsel on carbohydrate counting, monitor capillary glucose TID.
3. **Ulcerative colitis flare.**
   - Continue mesalamine 4 g TID.
   - Continue prednisone taper; coordinate with rheumatology for steroid‑sparing agent if flare recurs.
4. **High ileostomy output.**
   - High‑protein, low‑fat diet; consider octreotide 50 µg SC BID if output >3 L/day.
5. **Post‑op anemia and nutritional status.**
   - Repeat CBC in one week; consider iron supplementation if ferritin <40 ng/mL.
6. **Infection monitoring.**
   - Daily temperature, wound inspection; CRP and WBC trend.

**Follow‑up Recommendations:**
- Infectious Disease consult in 7 days or earlier if fever or leukocytosis worsens.
- Surgical Oncology follow‑up in 2 weeks for stoma care and wound status.
- Endocrinology review in 4 weeks to reassess insulin regimen.
- Primary care visit in 6 weeks for overall status.
- Patient education: wound care, signs of peritonitis, hyperglycemia warnings, medication adherence.

The patient expressed anxiety about his infection and postoperative pain; addressed with reassurance, pain control (acetaminophen 1 g PRN) and wound care education.

• CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (2025‑02‑27): 4.5 cm infiltrative focus in the cecum extending into adjacent terminal ileum, enlarged para‑mesenteric lymph node 1.2 cm; no distant disease.

Assessment:
1. Possible colorectal carcinoma involving cecum and ileum – imaging suggests malignant‑appearing mass; endoscopic evaluation pending.
2. Active ulcerative colitis flare – clinical history, elevated CRP, and high‑output ileostomy output support ongoing inflammation.
3. Iron deficiency anemia secondary to chronic disease / GI blood loss.
4. High‑output ileostomy causing dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, hyperglycemia.

Plan:
1. Initiate adjuvant chemotherapy: capecitabine 650 mg/m² PO BID (two days on/one day off) and bevacizumab 7.5 mg/kg IV q14 days.
2. Supportive measures:
   • Oral iron 325 mg PO daily.
   • Diuretics (furosemide 20 mg PO daily) for edema.
   • Continue prednisone 30 mg daily with taper plan; monitor for side effects.
   • Continue mesalamine 4 g BID.
   • Continue Metformin 500 mg BID, basal insulin glargine 10 units nightly adjusted for high output.
   • Lisinopril 20 mg QD, Atorvastatin 40 mg QHS.
3. Hydration: encourage 2–3 L fluids; consider oral rehydration salts for high‑output.
4. Monitor electrolytes, renal function, and glucose daily.
5. Referral for flexible sigmoidoscopy/colonoscopy to obtain biopsy of mass before starting chemotherapy. Schedule as soon as clinically feasible.
6. Wound care: continue dressing changes, monitor for SSI recurrence.
7. Dietitian consult to address high‑output ileostomy nutrition.
8. Follow‑up visit in 2 weeks to review labs, assess tolerance to chemo, and adjust steroids.

timeline.note:
Chief Complaint: Multidisciplinary discussion of recurrent colorectal carcinoma and evaluation of palliative treatment options.

Patient: 70‑year‑old male with a history of ulcerative colitis (pancolitis), type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obesity, and a post‑operative ileostomy.

Presenting Status: No acute symptoms reported during this board meeting. The patient remained clinically stable following recent postoperative complications (superficial surgical site infection, ileostomy‑related hyperglycemia, and ulcerative colitis flare). Current vitals were not documented during the board discussion.

Recent Findings:
- CT abdomen/pelvis: New cecal mass consistent with recurrence, stage III with nodal involvement.
- Post‑operative course: Superficial wound infection (treated with vancomycin), high‑output ileostomy, hyperglycemia requiring adjustment of insulin and metformin, ulcerative colitis flare controlled with mesalamine and prednisone taper.

Multidisciplinary Consensus:
- Full adjuvant FOLFOX regimen deemed intolerable given age, frailty, surgical complications, and risk of cumulative toxicity.
- Repeat surgical re‑section poses high risk of peritoneal seeding and offers limited benefit over systemic therapy.
- Limited benefit anticipated from additional radiotherapy in stage III nodal disease.
- Recommended initiation of modest palliative chemotherapy with capecitabine + bevacizumab, with close monitoring of chemotherapy‑related toxicities and wound status.
- Emphasized aggressive nutritional support and monitoring of stoma output.
- No further surgical intervention will be pursued unless a clear clinical benefit emerges.

Assessment & Plan:
1. **Recurrent stage III colorectal carcinoma (nodal involvement)** – initiate palliative chemotherapy.
   - Capecitabine 2000 mg/m2 PO BID for 14 days, then 7‑day rest (cycle 1). Subsequent cycles to be considered based on tolerance.
   - Bevacizumab 7.5 mg/kg IV Q21d.
2. Continue baseline medications: metformin 500 mg BID, basal insulin glargine 10 units nightly, lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, prednisone taper per schedule, mesalamine 4 g BID, and vancomycin C/D as ordered.
3. Monitor peripheral CBC, CMP, LFTs, and lipid panel prior to each chemotherapy cycle.
4. Watch for capecitabine toxicity (hand‑foot syndrome, mucositis) and bevacizumab complications (hypertension, bleeding, thromboembolism). Adjust doses per toxicity.
5. Nutritional assessment: high‑protein, high‑calorie diet; referral to dietitian for ileostomy‑specific nutrition; monitor weight objectively.
6. Stoma output: schedule weekly assessment; educate patient on fluid and electrolyte management; consider antidiarrheal agents if output remains high.
7. Wound surveillance: weekly wound checks; prompt reporting of redness, drainage, or pain.
8. Endocrinology re‑involvement to adjust insulin regimen in light of capecitabine‑induced hyperglycemia.
9. Tumor board re‑evaluation at 3 months or sooner if clinical status changes.
10. Patient education: signs of infection, DVT/PE, severe mucositis, and steroid side effects; ensure adherence to follow‑up and reporting.

Overall, this plan balances potential for symptomatic palliation against the patient’s frailty and comorbidities, providing a realistic, patient‑centered approach to recurrent disease management.
relevant_history: CT evidence of cecal recurrence, Stage III
timeline.visit_scenario: A multidisciplinary tumor board (oncology, colorectal surgery, gastroenterology, radiology, oncology nursing, palliative care) convened to evaluate the recurrent cancer. Surgical re‑section posed high peritoneal seeding risk, and the benefit of additional radiation in a stage III with nodal involvement is limited. The board recommended initiating modest palliative chemotherapy with capecitabine + bevacizumab, with close monitoring for toxicity. The board also emphasized intensive nutritional support and close follow‑up for wound status and stoma output. No further surgical intervention would be pursued unless clear clinical benefit emerged.

timeline.reason_for_visit: Transition to comfort‑focused care and hospice planning
timeline.note:
**Subjective:**
The patient is a 70‑year‑old male with a history of ulcerative colitis (pancolitis), metastatic colorectal carcinoma, type 2 diabetes, hypertension, obesity, and chronic heart disease, who now presents for a discussion about transition to comfort‑focused care. 

He reports worsening abdominal pain (RLQ, moderate to severe), shortness of breath on minimal exertion, significant peripheral edema, and a marked decrease in appetite. He has persistent high ileostomy output (>2 liters/day). The patient states, “I just want to be at ease.” The family has expressed a desire for a goal‑oriented approach emphasizing comfort and has declined further aggressive oncologic interventions.

**Objective:**
- Vitals: BP 118/70, HR 92, RR 18, SpO₂ 96% RA, Temp 98.2 °F.
- General: Pale, mild pallor, dry mucous membranes.
- Abdomen: Soft, mild RLQ tenderness, no rebound or guarding. High ileostomy output noted.
- Extremities: Bilateral pitting edema to mid‑shins.
- Respiratory: Clear to auscultation, no wheezes; dyspnea on minimal activity.

**Assessment:**
The patient is in a trajectory of declining functional status with multiple organ involvement. Labs demonstrate ongoing anemia (Hb 8.2 g/dL), significant inflammation (CRP 15 mg/L), and hypoalbuminemia, consistent with chronic disease and malnutrition. The high ileostomy output likely contributes to fluid loss, electrolyte disturbances, and hyperglycemia. 

Key clinical considerations:
1. Progressive metastatic colorectal carcinoma – not amenable to curative or cytotoxic therapy at this time.
2. Ulcerative colitis flare – ongoing colonic inflammation contributing to anemia and abdominal pain.
3. Anemia of chronic disease/potential blood loss.
4. Congestive heart failure exacerbation – peripheral edema, dyspnea.
5. Capecitabine & bevacizumab toxicity (diarrhea, mucositis, bleeding potential).
6. Palliative care goals – emphasis on symptom control, dignity, and family comfort.

**Plan:**
- **Chemotherapy:** Discontinue capecitabine and bevacizumab immediately.
- **Pain Management:** Initiate morphine 10 mg PO q4h PRN; titrate to effect. Consider hydromorphone 2 mg PO q4h PRN if inadequate.
- **Nausea/Vomiting:** Ondansetron 8 mg PO q8h PRN.
- **Appetite:** Megestrol acetate 500 mg PO daily.
- **Edema / Fluid Balance:** Continue loop diuretic 20 mg PO daily; monitor intake/output, daily weights.
- **Anemia:** Consider erythropoiesis‑stimulating agent (epoetin β 40 µg SC weekly) if symptomatic and stable; otherwise, transfuse PRBCs as needed.
- **Ileostomy Care:** Home stoma nurse visit scheduled; educate family on output monitoring and compression therapy.
- **Symptom Monitoring:** Daily phone call first week, then twice weekly home visits by hospice nurse.
- **Hospice Enrollment:** Formal hospice admission prior to discharge. Coordinate with hospice home‑care team for pain, dyspnea, and wound care.
- **Family Support:** Social work and spiritual care referrals.
- **Follow‑up:** Contact the palliative care clinic within 24 h of any escalation of pain (>5/10) or new respiratory distress. Next in‑clinic visit: 2 weeks after enrollment if clinically indicated.

The goals are to maximize comfort, preserve dignity, and support the patient-family unit during the final phase of life.

Chief Complaint: 70‑year‑old male presents with vague fatigue, lower right abdominal pressure, and darker stools over the past six months, accompanied by a 10‑lb unintentional weight loss. 
History of Present Illness: Mr. Martin reports feeling generally fatigued and experiencing a pressure sensation in the right lower quadrant. He notes darker stools with occasional faint red tint but denies frank rectal bleeding. He has a 12‑year history of type 2 diabetes, well‑controlled on metformin, hypertension on lisinopril, and obesity (BMI 33.5). He has a 30‑pack‑year smoking history and a family history of colorectal cancer (father, age 68). He has ulcerative colitis (pancolitis) diagnosed at age 36 and denies recent medication adherence. 
Past Medical History: Ulcerative colitis, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obesity. 
Medications: Metformin 500 mg BID, lisinopril 10 mg daily. 
Allergies: None. 
Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue, weight loss, and altered stool color. No abdominal pain beyond mild pressure, no nausea/vomiting, no fever, or rectal bleeding. 
Physical Examination: Vitals: BP 138/82, HR 88, Temp 98.4 °F, RR 16, SpO₂ 98% on room air. General: warm, alert, no acute distress. Abdomen: mild tenderness in the right lower quadrant; no rebound or guarding, no masses. No stigmata of malnutrition. 
Labs: CBC shows mild normocytic anemia (Hb 11.4 g/dL, Hct 34%); CMP is within normal limits. HbA1c 7.2% indicates borderline hyperglycemia. Fecal calprotectin is elevated at 300 µg/g, supporting active colonic inflammation. 
Assessment: 1) Ulcerative colitis flare – clinical presentation and fecal calprotectin suggest active pancolitis. 2) Possible occult GI bleeding or colonic lesion given dark stools and family history of colorectal cancer; colonoscopy is indicated to rule out malignancy or severe mucosal disease. 3) Mild anemia likely anemia of chronic disease or chronic GI blood loss. 4) Moderately controlled diabetes – HbA1c 7.2% needs monitoring. 
Plan: 
- Initiate prednisone 40 mg PO daily, taper over 2 weeks to minimize steroid exposure. 
- Start mesalamine 4 g PO BID to control colonic inflammation. 
- Counsel patient on adherence, steroid side‑effect monitoring, and importance of early reporting of worsening abdominal pain, bleeding, or fever. 
- Schedule colonoscopy within 4–6 weeks to evaluate mucosal inflammation, rule out neoplasia, and assess bleeding source. 
- Repeat CBC and CMP in 2 weeks to monitor anemia and renal function, and repeat fecal calprotectin in 6 weeks to gauge response. 
- Follow‑up clinic visit in 2 weeks to assess symptom improvement and medication tolerance. 
- Discuss lifestyle modifications; advise smoking cessation support, dietician referral for weight management, and diabetes education. 
- Educate about signs of complications: severe abdominal pain, persistent bleeding, high fever, or signs of steroid toxicity. 
Disposition: Discharged home with prescriptions, follow‑up appointments, and instructions for urgent care if symptoms worsen.

---
**Chief Complaint:** Pre‑operative diabetes optimization

**History of Present Illness:**
Mr. Martin, 70‑year‑old male with type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obesity, and pancolitis, presents for pre‑operative endocrine optimization prior to scheduled right hemicolectomy on 2025‑02‑18. He reports ongoing fatigue, increased thirst, and polyuria, but denies new gastrointestinal complaints. He has been on insulin glargine 20 units daily plus metformin 1000 mg BID. Recent labs reveal HbA1c 7.2% and fasting glucose 148 mg/dL.

**Review of Systems:**
- Constitutional: fatigue
- Genitourinary: polyuria, increased thirst
- GI: no new symptoms
- Cardiovascular: no chest pain, stable BP
- Respiratory: no SOB

**Past Medical History:**
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus
- Hypertension
- Obesity (BMI 33.5)
- Ulcerative colitis, pancolitis, on prednisone taper (40 mg daily, taper pending)
- Active colitis flare; recent colonoscopy with 4‑cm right colon mass

**Past Surgical History:**
- None prior to planned hemicolectomy

**Medications:**
- Metformin 1000 mg BID (prior), now 500 mg BID
- Insulin glargine 20 units daily (prior), now 10 units nightly
- Lisinopril 20 mg daily
- Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly
- Mesalamine 4 g BID
- Prednisone 40 mg daily (taper pending)
- Cefazolin 1 g IV (pre‑op prophylaxis)
- Nicotine patch 1 patch/day (prescribed for smoking cessation)

**Allergies:** None known.

**Social History:**
- Former smoker, 40 pack‑year history; currently using nicotine patch.
- No alcohol or illicit drug use.

**Vitals:**
- BP: 132/74 mmHg
- HR: 80 bpm
- Temp: 98.6 °F
- RR: 15/min
- SpO₂: 98% on room air
- BMI: 33.5

**Physical Exam:**
- General: alert, in no acute distress
- HEENT: normal
- Cardiovascular: regular rate/rhythm, no murmurs
- Respiratory: clear to auscultation
- Abdominal: soft, nondistended, no tenderness
- Extremities: no pedal edema, peripheral pulses palpable
- Neurologic: grossly intact

**Assessment:**
1. Type 2 diabetes mellitus – currently suboptimally controlled (HbA1c 7.2%, fasting glucose 148 mg/dL). Peri‑operative hyperglycemia risk is elevated due to ongoing prednisone therapy and surgical stress.
2. Hypertension – well controlled on lisinopril.
3. Obesity – BMI 33.5 contributes to insulin resistance.
4. Ulcerative colitis – active flare on prednisone taper; risk of infection with peri‑operative antibiotics.
5. Smoking – high peri‑operative risk; nicotine patch prescribed.

**Plan:**
- **Diabetes management:**
  - Reduce metformin to 500 mg BID (to minimize GI side effects and renal safety pre‑op). 
  - Initiate basal insulin glargine 10 units nightly (titrated from 20 units daily) to improve insulin sensitivity and achieve peri‑operative glucose targets of 80–180 mg/dL.
  - Provide patient education on carbohydrate counting, insulin timing, and hypoglycemia awareness.
  - Arrange dietitian consult to increase protein intake and reduce simple sugars.
- **Blood pressure:** Continue lisinopril 20 mg; monitor BP during pre‑op period.
- **Prednisone taper:** Continue 40 mg daily until 3 days pre‑op, then taper to 20 mg for 2 days with goal of <20 mg by surgery day.
- **Smoking cessation:** Prescribe nicotine patch; reinforce counseling.
- **Pre‑operative labs:** Repeat fasting glucose and HbA1c one week pre‑op; obtain CMP to confirm normal renal function.
- **Surgical coordination:** Confirm timing of right hemicolectomy (2025‑02‑18) and ensure antibiotic prophylaxis (cefazolin 1 g IV) per surgical protocol.
- **Education:** Discuss expected peri‑operative glucose fluctuations, insulin adjustments, and monitoring.

**Follow‑up recommendations:**
- Return for diabetes follow‑up 1 week prior to surgery (2025‑02‑11) to re‑measure fasting glucose and adjust insulin dose as needed.
- Continue dietary modifications and nicotine patch until at least surgery day.
- Post‑operative endocrine follow‑up within 48 hrs after surgery to adjust insulin and review glucose logs.
- Coordinate with surgical oncology for operative day medication schedule.
- If HbA1c or fasting glucose are markedly elevated (>200 mg/dL), delay surgery and repeat labs.

notes.content:
**Chief Complaint:** Elective laparoscopic right hemicolectomy with protective loop ileostomy.

**History of Present Illness:**
A 70‑year‑old male with a long history of pancolitis, T2DM, HTN, obesity, and well‑controlled medications underwent elective laparoscopic right hemicolectomy with creation of a protective loop ileostomy on 2025‑02‑18. Resection margins were clear. Post‑operative course was complicated by a superficial surgical site infection (SSI) noted on POD 3 with erythema and drainage. Percutaneous drainage of the incision was performed on POD 5 and empiric vancomycin 1 g IV BID was started for 5 days. Ileostomy output remained high, contributing to hyperglycemia with a peak glucose of 250 mg/dL on POD 7. Insulin and metformin doses were adjusted accordingly. The patient developed a flare of ulcerative colitis (low‑grade fever 38.4 °C, leukocytosis 12.5×10⁹/L) on POD 10; mesalamine was increased and a small‑dose prednisone taper was initiated. A repeat abdominal ultrasound on POD 10 excluded an abscess. Infectious disease (ID) consulted and recommended a longer course of IV antibiotics due to concern for seeding of the peritoneal cavity. The patient spent 4 days in ICU and was transferred to the surgical ward once stabilized. By POD 14 the patient ambulated with support and was discharged home with wound care and nutrition instructions.

**Past Medical History:** Ulcerative colitis (pancolitis), Type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obesity.

**Medications (pre‑discharge):**
- Metformin 500 mg BID
- Insulin glargine 10 units nightly
- Lisinopril 20 mg daily
- Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly
- Prednisone 40 mg daily (taper ongoing: 40 mg day 1‑3, 30 mg day 4‑7, 20 mg day 8‑10, 10 mg day 11‑14)
- Mesalamine 4 g BID (increased to 4 g TID for flare)
- Cefazolin 1 g IV pre‑op
- Vancomycin 1 g IV BID (currently 5 days)
- Nicotine patch

**Allergies:** NKDA

**Vitals (Day 14):** BP 140/80, HR 96, T 37.8 °C, RR 18, SpO₂ 97 %

**Physical Examination:**
- Incision: tibial, erythematous, drainage present.
- Ileostomy: high output, no abdominal guarding or peritonitis.
- Right lower quadrant: mild tenderness without rebound.

**Labs and Tests:**
- CBC: WBC 12.5×10⁹/L
- BMP: within normal limits
- CRP: 20 mg/L
- Blood cultures: negative
- Peritoneal fluid culture: negative
- Ultrasound: no abscess

**Assessment and Plan:**
1. **Post‑operative superficial SSI with potential peritoneal seeding.**
   - Continue IV antibiotic coverage: Vancomycin 1 g IV BID (extend to 14 days total) plus Ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily for broad coverage.
   - Re‑evaluate wound drainage daily; repeat ultrasound if clinical deterioration.
2. **Diabetes mellitus exacerbated by ileostomy output.**
   - Increase insulin glargine to 15 units nightly; add NPH 10 units BID if glucose >200 mg/dL.
   - Counsel on carbohydrate counting, monitor capillary glucose TID.
3. **Ulcerative colitis flare.**
   - Continue mesalamine 4 g TID.
   - Continue prednisone taper; coordinate with rheumatology for steroid‑sparing agent if flare recurs.
4. **High ileostomy output.**
   - High‑protein, low‑fat diet; consider octreotide 50 µg SC BID if output >3 L/day.
5. **Post‑op anemia and nutritional status.**
   - Repeat CBC in one week; consider iron supplementation if ferritin <40 ng/mL.
6. **Infection monitoring.**
   - Daily temperature, wound inspection; CRP and WBC trend.

**Follow‑up Recommendations:**
- Infectious Disease consult in 7 days or earlier if fever or leukocytosis worsens.
- Surgical Oncology follow‑up in 2 weeks for stoma care and wound status.
- Endocrinology review in 4 weeks to reassess insulin regimen.
- Primary care visit in 6 weeks for overall status.
- Patient education: wound care, signs of peritonitis, hyperglycemia warnings, medication adherence.

The patient expressed anxiety about his infection and postoperative pain; addressed with reassurance, pain control (acetaminophen 1 g PRN) and wound care education.

Chief Complaint: Multidisciplinary discussion of recurrent colorectal carcinoma and evaluation of palliative treatment options.

Patient: 70‑year‑old male with a history of ulcerative colitis (pancolitis), type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obesity, and a post‑operative ileostomy.

Presenting Status: No acute symptoms reported during this board meeting. The patient remained clinically stable following recent postoperative complications (superficial surgical site infection, ileostomy‑related hyperglycemia, and ulcerative colitis flare). Current vitals were not documented during the board discussion.

Recent Findings:
- CT abdomen/pelvis: New cecal mass consistent with recurrence, stage III with nodal involvement.
- Post‑operative course: Superficial wound infection (treated with vancomycin), high‑output ileostomy, hyperglycemia requiring adjustment of insulin and metformin, ulcerative colitis flare controlled with mesalamine and prednisone taper.

Multidisciplinary Consensus:
- Full adjuvant FOLFOX regimen deemed intolerable given age, frailty, surgical complications, and risk of cumulative toxicity.
- Repeat surgical re‑section poses high risk of peritoneal seeding and offers limited benefit over systemic therapy.
- Limited benefit anticipated from additional radiotherapy in stage III nodal disease.
- Recommended initiation of modest palliative chemotherapy with capecitabine + bevacizumab, with close monitoring of chemotherapy‑related toxicities and wound status.
- Emphasized aggressive nutritional support and monitoring of stoma output.
- No further surgical intervention will be pursued unless a clear clinical benefit emerges.

Assessment & Plan:
1. **Recurrent stage III colorectal carcinoma (nodal involvement)** – initiate palliative chemotherapy.
   - Capecitabine 2000 mg/m2 PO BID for 14 days, then 7‑day rest (cycle 1). Subsequent cycles to be considered based on tolerance.
   - Bevacizumab 7.5 mg/kg IV Q21d.
2. Continue baseline medications: metformin 500 mg BID, basal insulin glargine 10 units nightly, lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, prednisone taper per schedule, mesalamine 4 g BID, and vancomycin C/D as ordered.
3. Monitor peripheral CBC, CMP, LFTs, and lipid panel prior to each chemotherapy cycle.
4. Watch for capecitabine toxicity (hand‑foot syndrome, mucositis) and bevacizumab complications (hypertension, bleeding, thromboembolism). Adjust doses per toxicity.
5. Nutritional assessment: high‑protein, high‑calorie diet; referral to dietitian for ileostomy‑specific nutrition; monitor weight objectively.
6. Stoma output: schedule weekly assessment; educate patient on fluid and electrolyte management; consider antidiarrheal agents if output remains high.
7. Wound surveillance: weekly wound checks; prompt reporting of redness, drainage, or pain.
8. Endocrinology re‑involvement to adjust insulin regimen in light of capecitabine‑induced hyperglycemia.
9. Tumor board re‑evaluation at 3 months or sooner if clinical status changes.
10. Patient education: signs of infection, DVT/PE, severe mucositis, and steroid side effects; ensure adherence to follow‑up and reporting.

Overall, this plan balances potential for symptomatic palliation against the patient’s frailty and comorbidities, providing a realistic, patient‑centered approach to recurrent disease management.

notes.content:
**Subjective:**
The patient is a 70‑year‑old male with a history of ulcerative colitis (pancolitis), metastatic colorectal carcinoma, type 2 diabetes, hypertension, obesity, and chronic heart disease, who now presents for a discussion about transition to comfort‑focused care. 

He reports worsening abdominal pain (RLQ, moderate to severe), shortness of breath on minimal exertion, significant peripheral edema, and a marked decrease in appetite. He has persistent high ileostomy output (>2 liters/day). The patient states, “I just want to be at ease.” The family has expressed a desire for a goal‑oriented approach emphasizing comfort and has declined further aggressive oncologic interventions.

**Objective:**
- Vitals: BP 118/70, HR 92, RR 18, SpO₂ 96% RA, Temp 98.2 °F.
- General: Pale, mild pallor, dry mucous membranes.
- Abdomen: Soft, mild RLQ tenderness, no rebound or guarding. High ileostomy output noted.
- Extremities: Bilateral pitting edema to mid‑shins.
- Respiratory: Clear to auscultation, no wheezes; dyspnea on minimal activity.

**Assessment:**
The patient is in a trajectory of declining functional status with multiple organ involvement. Labs demonstrate ongoing anemia (Hb 8.2 g/dL), significant inflammation (CRP 15 mg/L), and hypoalbuminemia, consistent with chronic disease and malnutrition. The high ileostomy output likely contributes to fluid loss, electrolyte disturbances, and hyperglycemia. 

Key clinical considerations:
1. Progressive metastatic colorectal carcinoma – not amenable to curative or cytotoxic therapy at this time.
2. Ulcerative colitis flare – ongoing colonic inflammation contributing to anemia and abdominal pain.
3. Anemia of chronic disease/potential blood loss.
4. Congestive heart failure exacerbation – peripheral edema, dyspnea.
5. Capecitabine & bevacizumab toxicity (diarrhea, mucositis, bleeding potential).
6. Palliative care goals – emphasis on symptom control, dignity, and family comfort.

**Plan:**
- **Chemotherapy:** Discontinue capecitabine and bevacizumab immediately.
- **Pain Management:** Initiate morphine 10 mg PO q4h PRN; titrate to effect. Consider hydromorphone 2 mg PO q4h PRN if inadequate.
- **Nausea/Vomiting:** Ondansetron 8 mg PO q8h PRN.
- **Appetite:** Megestrol acetate 500 mg PO daily.
- **Edema / Fluid Balance:** Continue loop diuretic 20 mg PO daily; monitor intake/output, daily weights.
- **Anemia:** Consider erythropoiesis‑stimulating agent (epoetin β 40 µg SC weekly) if symptomatic and stable; otherwise, transfuse PRBCs as needed.
- **Ileostomy Care:** Home stoma nurse visit scheduled; educate family on output monitoring and compression therapy.
- **Symptom Monitoring:** Daily phone call first week, then twice weekly home visits by hospice nurse.
- **Hospice Enrollment:** Formal hospice admission prior to discharge. Coordinate with hospice home‑care team for pain, dyspnea, and wound care.
- **Family Support:** Social work and spiritual care referrals.
- **Follow‑up:** Contact the palliative care clinic within 24 h of any escalation of pain (>5/10) or new respiratory distress. Next in‑clinic visit: 2 weeks after enrollment if clinically indicated.

The goals are to maximize comfort, preserve dignity, and support the patient-family unit during the final phase of life.
medications: Morphine sulfate 10 mg PO q4h PRN
Erythropoiesis‑stimulating agent (epoetin β) 40 µg SC weekly (if indicated)
Hydromorphone 2 mg PO q4h PRN (if morphine inadequate)

Schedule home stoma nurse visit

Inform family to contact palliative care for pain >5/10 or respiratory distress
Provide social work and spiritual counseling

prior_visit_summaries: Patient, 70‑year‑old male, reported fatigue, right lower quadrant pressure, dark stools, and 10‑lb weight loss over 6 months. Examination revealed mild RLQ tenderness; vitals were stable. Labs showed mild normocytic anemia, HbA1c 7.2%, and fecal calprotectin 300 µg/g. Prednisone 40 mg daily was started with a two‑week taper and mesalamine 4 g BID was initiated. Colonoscopy was scheduled for 4–6 weeks, and the patient was instructed on steroid side‑effect monitoring and a follow‑up visit in two weeks.

Follow‑up visit on 2025‑01‑29 after colonoscopy revealed diffuse erythema, erosions, and a 4 cm ceacal polypoid lesion. Active ulcerative colitis flare ongoing. Prednisone taper continued; mesalamine continued. Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly initiated. Scheduled 2‑week re‑evaluation after pathology; CBC/CMP/LFT monitoring. No new abdominal tenderness.

Patient, 70‑year‑old male, presents for post‑colonoscopy staging. Contrast CT abdomen/pelvis shows a 4‑cm localized right colon mass with no lymphadenopathy or metastasis. He continues prednisone 40 mg daily on taper and mesalamine 4 g BID; insulin glargine 20 units daily initiated for pre‑operative glucose control. Pre‑operative antibiotic prophylaxis cefazolin 1 g IV ordered. Surgical oncology consult scheduled for 2025‑02‑12; laparoscopic right hemicolectomy planned after optimization. Labs (CBC, CMP, LFTs, HbA1c) to be obtained before surgery.

2025‑02‑15 Endocrinology visit for pre‑operative diabetes optimization. Patient reported fatigue, polyuria, and increased thirst. Vital signs stable (BP 132/74 mmHg, HR 80 bpm, BMI 33.5). Medications adjusted: Metformin reduced to 500 mg BID, insulin glargine changed to 10 units nightly, nicotine patch started. Prednisone taper continued. Basal insulin glargine initiated to improve peri‑operative glucose control. Pre‑operative labs ordered: fasting glucose, HbA1c, CMP. No new abdominal findings.

Elective laparoscopic right hemicolectomy with protective loop ileostomy performed. Resection margins clear. Post‑operative course complicated by a superficial surgical site infection on POD3, treated with vancomycin and percutaneous drainage on POD5. Ileostomy high output contributed to hyperglycemia; insulin and metformin adjusted. Ulcerative colitis flare on POD10, managed with increased mesalamine and prednisone taper. Repeat abdominal ultrasound excluded abscess. 4 days ICU stay, discharged home on POD14 with wound care and nutrition instructions.

Tumor board meeting 2025‑02‑27; discussion of recurrent stage III colorectal carcinoma with nodal involvement; initiation of modest palliative chemotherapy with capecitabine 2000 mg/m² BID 14 days on/7 days off and bevacizumab 7.5 mg/kg IV every 21 days; continuation of metformin, insulin glargine, lisinopril, atorvastatin, prednisone taper, mesalamine, and vancomycin C/D; monitoring CBC/CMP/LFTs, wound status, stoma output, diabetes control; no surgical intervention planned; multidisciplinary follow‑up at 3 months.

2025‑04‑30 Palliative Care visit: patient reports worsening abdominal pain, dyspnea, peripheral edema, and high ileostomy output; vitals stable, Hb 8.2 g/dL, CRP 15 mg/L, hypoalbuminemia; started morphine 10 mg PO q4h PRN, ondansetron 8 mg PO q8h PRN, megestrol acetate 500 mg PO daily, epoetinβ 40 µg SC weekly if indicated; discontinued capecitabine and bevacizumab; plan hospice enrollment, home stoma nurse visit, daily phone check‑in, and supportive care.
